petitioner_background: The petitioner is a rhythmic gymnastics coach from Russia who has been serving as a rhythmic gymnastics instructor at the Specialized Youth Sports School of Olympic Reserve #5 in Astrakhan City, Russia since 1994. She sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics. She was age twenty-five at the time of filing (May 29, 2003), and her prior career as a competitive rhythmic gymnast ended in the early 1990s (last competed in 1991). She is the daughter of a World Champion in Gymnastics and intends to work as a coach in the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) a letter from G.E. Lukina, Director of the Specialized Youth Sports School of Olympic Reserve #5, stating that in 1987 petitioner became a winner of an all-union tournament and in 1991 became a champion of Russia in group routine and a Master of Sport; (2) an October 1987 certificate from the Department of Education of the Russian Republic of the Soviet Union stating she won first place in the Rhythmic Gymnastics Championship of the Soviet Union among juniors (when she was only 10 years old); (3) a photocopy of a first place medal alleged to be from the 1991 Russian National Championships (a team award, when she was 14); (4) an identification certificate from the Government Committee of the USSR for Culture and Sport reflecting her designation as 'Master of Sports' in rhythmic gymnastics (#268,009); (5) letters from Irina Chaplygina stating she received a bronze medal at the European Championship in group routine in 1997 in Greece and was champion at an international tournament in Bulgaria in 1998 under petitioner's supervision; (6) evidence that Chaplygina received 'a bronze for Europe' at the Senior AA level at the fifth annual San Francisco Rhythmic Invitational in 1998; (7) a letter from Olga Kapranova stating she became World Champion among club teams on junior level in 2000 in Japan and earned the title 'Master of Sports on International Level' under petitioner's supervision; (8) evidence that Kapranova received the highest all-around score at Junior AA level at the San Francisco Rhythmic Invitational in 1998; (9) official results from five rhythmic gymnastics tournaments in Russia in 2004 (exhibits J, K, L, M, N); (10) a letter from the mother of Anastasia Torba about training from 2002-2005; (11) evidence that Torba placed third in the All-Around Junior Category at the 11th annual San Francisco Rhythmic Invitational in 2005.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. The 1987 junior-level award was not an indication of reaching the 'very top of the field of endeavor' as petitioner was only 10 years old and faced only her age group. The 1991 national championship was a team award and petitioner provided no first-hand evidence of scoring results showing she played a predominant or leading role in the team's victory, nor that the competition included athletes above the junior level (she was 14). Regarding the Master of Sport designation, no first-hand evidence of official requirements was provided — counsel's unsupported assertions do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez). Additionally, these athletic awards were based on her ability as an athlete, not as a coach. Under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), awards won by athletes she coached could be considered as comparable evidence, but a coach of intermediates or junior-level athletes does not have a credible claim. Evidence showing she accompanied Chaplygina as her primary coach was missing. Kapranova's achievements were at the Junior level, not the highest level. The 2004 tournament results and 2005 Torba evidence came into existence subsequent to the filing date and cannot establish eligibility (8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12); Matter of Katigbak). Other than Chaplygina's success in 1997-1998, no evidence showed other gymnasts under petitioner's tutelage won national or international titles at the Senior level in a sustained manner leading to the May 29, 2003 filing date.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A November 9, 2004 letter from the Vice President of the National Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics certifying that petitioner is a member of the Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics of Astrakhan Region and is a member of the National Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics as per Contract of January 5, 2004. Also a letter (exhibit R) from the Specialized Youth Sports School of Olympic Reserve #5 explaining that membership in the National Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics is awarded only to coaches whose students have received a gold, silver, or bronze medal in the Russian National Championship, European Championship or World Championship competitions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence showing petitioner held membership in either organization as of the petition's filing date (May 29, 2003). New circumstances that did not exist as of the filing date cannot retroactively establish eligibility (8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12); Matter of Katigbak). The record contained no evidence of bylaws or official membership requirements for the Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics of the Astrakhan Region or the National Federation of Rhythmic Gymnastics to demonstrate these organizations require outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission. Official membership information from the National Federation itself would be of greater probative value than the second-hand information from the petitioner's immediate employer. Nor was there evidence showing petitioner was evaluated by experts at the national or international level rather than the regional level.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: (1) A letter from the Physical Training and Sport Committee of the Administration of the Astrakhan region confirming petitioner took part in an International rhythmic gymnastic competition from October 28-31, 2001 as a referee; (2) three certifications (exhibits T, U, V) from Head Judges and Judges of the National Level stating petitioner served as a judge and assistant of the Head Judge at competitions held in January, May, and November of 2004; (3) a November 9, 2004 letter from the Vice President of the Rhythmic Gymnastics Federation of Russia stating that petitioner's certification documents as a Certified Judge State Category were being processed in the Awards Department of the Federal Agency of Athletics, Sports and Tourism of Russia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: For the 2001 competition, the record contained no further information about the competition or specific event categories judged. It was not shown that the events involved athletes at the senior level as opposed to junior, intermediate, or novice levels. A one-sentence discussion was inadequate to demonstrate national or international acclaim. The 2004 certifications came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date (8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12); Matter of Katigbak) and subsequent developments cannot retroactively establish eligibility. Petitioner offered no explanation as to how she was able to serve as a judge at events where athletes she coaches were among the competitors. There was no evidence she served as a judge on the same level as the individuals providing the certifications. Based on the November 2004 letter, petitioner was apparently not eligible to judge at the State Category or national level until some time after November 2004.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel stated that between 1992 and 1994 petitioner toured multiple countries performing in a gymnastics exhibition entitled 'Calisthenics Constellation' — an international exhibition designed by petitioner's mother (a World Champion in Gymnastics) to demonstrate the artistic features of rhythmic gymnastics along with other gymnastics disciplines at venues around the world.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: There was no evidence showing petitioner performed in such an exhibition subsequent to 1994, therefore she has not sustained her prior acclaim as a performer. The AAO has consistently found this criterion is more appropriate for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than public performers. Acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of appearing in public, but by attracting a substantial audience. The record contained no evidence that petitioner's performances drew record crowds, were regular sell-out performances, or resulted in greater audiences than similar performances without her. Additionally, petitioner seeks classification as a rhythmic gymnastics coach rather than as a gymnastics performer — publicly performing artistic gymnastic routines is not the field in which she seeks to continue working in the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted petitioner performed in a leading or critical role for the Specialized Youth Sports School of Olympic Reserve #5 in Astrakhan City, Russia. Evidence included: (1) exhibit 5, a letter from director G.E. Lukina stating that from 1994 petitioner has been working at SDUSHOR No. 5 as a rhythmic gymnastics coach and was training a school team for the Russia championship in December 2001; (2) counsel's response stating petitioner 'presently holds the position of Senior Instructor'; (3) a subsequent letter from G.E. Lukina (exhibit X) stating petitioner is trusted to train gymnasts for the Russian National Team, that there are 38 coaches at SYSOR and only five are in charge of training programs for the highest level gymnasts, and that petitioner's 'sparking coaching talent and artistic approach' helped her attain a leading role at one of the oldest and most respected gymnastic schools in Russia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the director's finding that the evidence was adequate for this criterion. The record did not indicate the date of petitioner's appointment to Senior Instructor — subsequent developments cannot retroactively establish eligibility as of the filing date (8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12); Matter of Katigbak). The record did not adequately document petitioner's role as coach from 1994 to 2003. G.E. Lukina did not indicate whether petitioner trains gymnasts for the junior or senior national team, and the evidence indicated she works primarily in the youth category. Lukina did not state petitioner is the head coach of School #5's overall rhythmic gymnastic training program. The vague statements did not adequately distinguish petitioner's role from that of other rhythmic gymnastics coaches at School #5. Petitioner did not provide official statistics showing that from 1994 to 2003 students under her direct tutelage consistently outperformed competitors from other specialized youth sports schools or won a greater percentage of championships at the senior national level. Petitioner did not establish she performed in a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization, or that her involvement earned her sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed. The AAO found that petitioner failed to demonstrate she met at least three of the regulatory criteria necessary to establish sustained national or international acclaim. The AAO emphasized that since petitioner's career as a competitive gymnast ended in the early 1990s and she had worked as a coach since 1994, she must show sustained acclaim based on her achievements as a coach rather than her prior reputation as an athlete (citing Lee v. Ziglar that competing and coaching are not the same area of expertise). The evidence was not persuasive that petitioner's achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at the national or international level, nor that she had risen to the very top of her field. The AAO also rejected counsel's argument regarding comparable evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), noting that comparable evidence is inappropriate where an alien is simply unable to meet three criteria, though it did accept prizes of athletes coached by petitioner as analogous evidence. The burden of proof under Section 291 of the Act was not met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues noted: (1) Significant portions of the petitioner's evidence came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date of May 29, 2003 (including 2004 tournament results, 2004 membership letters, 2004 judging certifications, 2005 Torba evidence), which cannot retroactively establish eligibility under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak; (2) the AAO noted that unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence; (3) the petitioner offered no explanation as to how she was able to serve as a judge at events where athletes she coaches were among the competitors, raising a potential conflict of interest concern.
